elements.sent-2411-001: Computer models are used as a way to explore complex physical systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Stationary Gaussian process emulators, with their accompanying uncertainty quantification, are popular surrogates for computer models.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, many computer models are not well represented by stationary Gaussian processes models.
elements.sent-2411-004: Deep Gaussian processes have been shown to be capable of capturing non-stationary behaviors and abrupt regime changes in the computer model response.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we explore the properties of two deep Gaussian process formulations within the context of computer model emulation.
elements.sent-2411-006: For one of these formulations, we introduce a new parameter that controls the amount of smoothness in the deep Gaussian process layers.
elements.sent-2411-007: We adapt a stochastic variational approach to inference for this model, allowing for prior specification and posterior exploration of the smoothness of the response surface.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our approach can be applied to a large class of computer models, and scales to arbitrarily large simulation designs.
elements.sent-2411-009: The proposed methodology was motivated by the need to emulate an astrophysical model of the formation of binary black hole mergers.
elements.sent-2411-010: Computer models, or simulators, are widely used as a way to explore complex physical systems.
elements.sent-2411-011: Frequently, a simulator is computationally expensive and only a limited number of model evaluations are available.
elements.sent-2411-012: In other settings, the computational model is relatively fast to evaluate, but is not broadly available \citep[e.g.][]{Kauf .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In either case, an emulator of the computer models is needed to act as a surrogate.
elements.sent-2411-014: Stationary Gaussian processes (GP) have become the conventional approach for deterministic computer model emulation \citep{Sacks,jones .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In many cases, the simulator response surface does not resemble a realization of a stationary GP.
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, deep Gaussian processes (DGP) have been proposed for non-parametric regression \citep{law,Dunlop , and computer model emulation \citep{ann,raja,rada,dw .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Large datasets are challenging for GPs and even more so for DGPs.
elements.sent-2411-018: The Vecchia approximation \citep{kat has been successfully implemented for DGPs in current Bayesian implementations \citep[e.g.][]{min2,sau2 - scaling to hundreds of thousands of data points but not necessarily millions as in our motivating application.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this work, we introduce a non-stationary DGP emulator which can be applied to a large class of complex computer models and scales to large simulation designs.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our first aim is to lay out some of the properties of DGPs for computer model emulation.
elements.sent-2411-021: We adapt the approach in \citet{Dunlop so that it can serve as an emulator for computer models, and introduce a new parameter (or parameters) that allows us to control the smoothness of the DGP layers.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We also develop a variational inference (VI) approach for the proposed DGP which allows for prior specification and posterior exploration of the smoothness of the response surface.
elements.sent-2411-023: The proposed methodology was motivated by the emulation of an astrophysical model (Compact Object Mergers: Population Astrophysics and Statistics or COMPAS) that simulates the formation of binary black holes (BBHs) \citep{Bar .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The paper is organized as follows: Section \ref{sec:app introduces the challenges encountered when emulating the COMPAS model.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Section \ref{sec:emu presents an overview of GP emulation and describes existing work for non-stationary GPs.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In Section \ref{sec:dgp , the notation of two broad forms of the DGP are generalized to emphasize their differences and linking properties to stationary GPs.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In Section \ref{sec:sdgp , the DGP emulator is proposed through modifying one of the forms, followed by illustration of a new property induced by our modifications.}
elements.sent-2411-028: We finish off this section with adapting a VI approach to our DGP emulator and providing a prediction method.
elements.sent-2411-029: In Sections \ref{sec:ill3 and \ref{num:com , our proposed method is illustrated in a synthetic example as well as emulation of the COMPAS model.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The application that motivated the proposed methodology was emulation of the COMPAS simulator of BBH mergers \citep{Bar .}
elements.sent-2411-032: BBHs are systems consisting of two black holes in close orbit around each other (Figure \ref{fig:fig0 ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: When two black holes merge, a gravitational wave signal is emitted and is measured by ground-based detectors \citep{Man .}
elements.sent-2411-034: During the merger, mass (i.e., the {\it{chirp mass ) is expelled.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The inputs to the COMPAS model describe the initial conditions of the star system (e.g., the mass of the most massive star) and parameters that govern physical processes.
elements.sent-2411-036: The output of interest is the chirp mass of the merger.
elements.sent-2411-037: Given the complexity of binary stellar evolution, many billions of BBHs may need to be simulated to perform an experiment that is sufficiently large to make scientific inferences.
elements.sent-2411-038: Consequently, a fast statistical emulator of the COMPAS model is desired.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[height=1.5in]{BBH1.png} \includegraphics[height=1.5in]{BBH2.png} \includegraphics[height=1.5in]{BBH3.png} \caption{Computer simulation of the black hole binary system GW150914. Credits: SXS (Simulating eXtreme Spacetimes) project} \label{fig:fig0}
elements.sent-2411-039: The first challenge for emulation in this setting is the presence of regions of discontinuities in the response surface of the chirp mass.
elements.sent-2411-040: That is, BBHs only form in some regions of the input space.
elements.sent-2411-041: If a BBH system does not form, then no chirp mass is observed.
elements.sent-2411-042: The portions of input space that result in non-zero outputs are unknown.
elements.sent-2411-043: It is assumed that a chirp mass is observed in a union of compact regions within the input space.
elements.sent-2411-044: The second challenge for emulation is that the simulation suite is large ($\approx 2 \times 10^6$), and only about $24\%$ result in BBH mergers \citep[e.g.][]{broe .}
elements.sent-2411-045: If millions of BBHs are needed, a large number of simulations need to be performed.
elements.sent-2411-046: So, a fast emulator of the COMPAS model will have to be adapted to the type of complexity that we have in this setting and contend with a large number of simulation runs.
elements.sent-2411-047: This motivated us to propose a new methodology for building a non-stationary emulator which can be used in a wide range of applications - including the COMPAS model.. [-40pt].
elements.sent-2411-048: The conventional approach to computer model emulation is to use a stationary GP \citep{Sacks .}

elements.sent-2411-057: Most approaches to non-stationary GP modeling can be viewed as either space-warping or covariance-modeling.
elements.sent-2411-058: In the former, the input space is warped so that the observations can be modeled as a stationary GP \citep[e.g.][]{Samp,Smit .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Alternatively, one can vary the correlation function over the input space to deal with non-stationarity \citep[e.g.][]{hig2,Paciorek .}
elements.sent-2411-060: In recent years, DGPs have been proposed to accommodate the non-stationary response surfaces, and in the next section we present two common formulations as generalizations of space-warping and covariance-modeling methods.

elements.sent-2411-212: In this paper, the DGP of \citet{Dunlop was investigated and modified.}
elements.sent-2411-213: This work was motivated by the application of emulation of COMPAS model, a binary population synthesis model that simulates the formation of binary black holes (BBHs).
elements.sent-2411-214: We proposed a non-stationary DGP emulator which can be adapted to the type of discontinuities found on the COMPAS response surface, and scales to the large number of simulation runs.
elements.sent-2411-215: Our approach can be applied to a large class of complex computer models, and scales to arbitrarily large simulation designs as well.
elements.sent-2411-216: We generalized the notation of two broad forms of DGP \citep{law,Dunlop to emphasize their differences and properties.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Specifically, we addressed exactly what the DGP forms would look like if the data were actually a realization of a stationary GP in two propositions.
elements.sent-2411-218: Some advantages of the DGP of \citet{Dunlop such as operating covariance functions on the original input space rather than the warped input space, led us to consider it in our framework.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Our DGP emulator was proposed by modifying the \citet{Dunlop formulation through introducing a new parameter that controls smoothness of the DGP layers.}
elements.sent-2411-220: The impact of the proposed parameter on the level of smoothness of the hidden layers was theoretically illustrated and numerically visualised through DGP realizations.
elements.sent-2411-221: Particularly, in Proposition \ref{pro:alpha , we proved that what is happening in the non-stationary covariance function as the proposed parameter $\alpha$ changes from zero to infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Doing inference on the DGP emulator using sampling methods such as MCMC is computationally infeasible in large designs.
elements.sent-2411-223: Hence, we employed a VI approach to overcome the computational issues and adapted it to be able to estimate the posterior distribution of the smoothness parameter $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-224: Specifically, we adapted the DVSI method introduced by \citet{Sal (i) to be suitable for the DGP model of \citet{Dunlop modified by our proposed parameter $\alpha$ and (ii) to our framework, emulation of deterministic computer models.}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Our modified approach allowed us to explore the posterior distribution of the smoothness of the model response surface and was demonstrated to preserve accuracy with uncertainty measures for arbitrary large designs.
elements.sent-2411-226: Further possible innovations to the DGP model of \citet{Dunlop could be through (i) specifying an $\alpha$ for each coordinate dimension of the input space and/or at each non-stationary layer separately, and (ii) changing the dimensionality between layers.}
elements.sent-2411-227: By combining methods (i) and (ii) new different variants of the DGP can be proposed.
elements.sent-2411-228: Although, these new DGP variants get the benefit of having multidimensional layers in the DGP from (\ref{eq:44 ) and explicitly non-stationary covariance functions in the DGP model through (\ref{eq:66 ), more complexities are introduced into the original model (\ref{eq:44 ) by adding extra unobserved latent variables in each dimension and adding extra parameters to be estimated.}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: We defer a more thorough investigation of these new variants to future work.. [-35pt].

elements.sent-2411-265: Therefore, the ELBO of the DGP can be formed as [-25pt] \begin{equation}\Lagr_{DGP}=\sum_{i=1}^{n_S} \EE_{q(\{f_n\}_{n=1}^{N},\alpha)}\Big(~log~\PP(y_i\vert f_{N}^i)~\Big) - KL\Big(q(u_1)\parallel \PP(u_1;Z_{1})\Big) [-10pt]\end{equation} \begin{equation}~~~~~~~- \EE_{q(\alpha)}\Big[ \sum_{n=2}^{N}KL\Big(q(u_n) \parallel \PP(u_n;Z_{n},\delta_{Z_n},\alpha)\Big)\Big]-KL\Big(q(\alpha) \parallel \PP(\alpha)\Big).\end{equation}.
